paragraphs.body: Cradled between the shin and the thigh, a humble hinge that quietly folds. It does not get the notability of the beating heart, it does not stretch like a big muscle, it does not get the prominence of the thinking brain, yet it carries us every step with a silent strength. It gives us the ability to leap to the skies and dip below. It gives a firm platform for our falls so we get back up. On its best day, it goes unnoticed; it bends gracefully and rises with veracity. It is a stoic servant to the body. It takes on damages of brutal scrapes and twists in unnatural ways, but sometimes, it cannot hold firm. The cuts and twists grow too much, and then it gives. There is a pop! You know it is over; you fall to the ground in insufferable pain. You have seen it a dozen times and heard about it plenty more. You never think it happened to you until it does. You slam your fist into the ground, knowing instantly what has happened. Tears trickle ever so slightly down your cheeks as you try to stay strong in the midst of defeat. A crowd gathers and mummers as all you can do is sit in your brokennesses.

annotations.comment: Great descriptive paragraph! It's surprising in all good ways.
annotations.context_left: the notability of the beating heart, it does not stretch like a big muscle, it does not get the

paragraphs.body: It takes nine months to recover from an Anterior Cruciate Ligament tear, commonly known as an ACL tear. It started in my bed recovery from the grueling surgery, and the doctor said you should squeeze your quad and lift your leg. The pain was terrible, but the worst part was I could not even lift my leg on my own. For the first few months, I would only do quad exercises while lying in a bed or on a table at Physical Therapy. All I do is countless reps of squeeze and lift, hoping to get your leg to work as one unit again. I eventually got off the crutches, which was when my PT physicians started with strength training. This is by far the most challenging part of the whole journey. My quad was at fifty percent of the strength of my healthy leg, and you need it at a hundred percent to get back to action. Every other day, I would spend two strenuous hours at the gym working on my legs. Once I was able to get a leg at about eighty percent of the healthy one, which was about 6 months in, I started running again. It was a weird adjustment to run again. It feels unreal and like you are learning a whole new motion again. It was very wonky, and I was surprised I did not know how to do something I had done my whole life. The final part of the recovery was to get my agility or side-to-side motion back to par. I needed to get my knee to make the same cuts and twists it has my whole life. This long, irritating, tiring, and torturing process is what it takes to return to the field.

annotations.text: It feels unreal and like you are learning a whole new motion again. It
It was very wonky, and I was surprised I did not know how to do something I had done my whole life.

annotations.context_left: I could not even lift my leg on my own. For the first few months, I would only do quad exercises

paragraphs.body: The word return comes from the Middle English word retouren, derived from the old French word retourner. Retourner is a combination of the prefix re, which is back, and tourner, which means to turn. Tourner derives from the Latin word tornare, meaning to turn. Returning to the original sense means to turn back or come back. The word returned intrigued me because it represented what my process was about. I have worked so hard to try to get back where I was. As I have gone through the recovery, though, I have realized one can't really ever return. Something is always different; for me, playing soccer, there is a fear. I never had to worry about how my knee moves when I play. Every time I step there, I hesitate, wondering if it will break on me again. I will not ever truly return to just being able to play.

annotations.text: I have realized one can't really ever return. Something is always different; for me, playing soccer, there is a fear. I never had to worry about how my knee moves when I play. Every time I step there, I hesitate, wondering if it will break on me again. I will not ever truly return to just being able to play.
annotations.comment: Great insights. I wonder if this notion is a kind of metaphor? Does the idea of returning relate to your larger life as well?

annotations.text: The word return comes from the Middle English word retouren, derived from the old French word retourner.

paragraphs.body: I walked onto the field as the bolling Louisiana sun hit my back. The crisp summer air sharpens my lungs. Each step feels heavier as I get closer to the center circle. Sweat drips down my skin as there is nothing but anticipation. My breathing intensifies, knowing what is about to come. I feel my heartbeat thumping throughout my body. I bend over to tie my shoe once more. My knee feels the flawless golf course grass. I loop my laces just as I have done thousands of times before. I put my hand against the fresh grass and switched to my other knee. I finish tying my shoes as the refs come onto the field. All the parents, coaches, and the 22 players on the field watched intently as the referee walked over. He had three balls, all identical to him. The balls were white with blue and red stripes around them. As he got to the center circle, he dropped one ball, went to one side, punted the ball to the goalie, and did the same for the other. He then pointed to the goalies, ensuring they were ready to play. I looked at my teammate who played next to me, and as we did before every game, we dapped each other up. I took one more deep breath, and the ref blew his whistle. I close my eyes and hear the screeching sound, and it is time to play.

annotations.text: I walked onto the field as the bolling Louisiana sun hit my back. The crisp summer air sharpens my lungs. Each step feels heavier as I get closer to the center circle. Sweat drips down my skin as there is nothing but anticipation.

paragraphs.body: Returning meaning is to come back to something. People return every day: returning home from work, returning to their favorite restaurant, returning to a favorite vacation spot, or returning home for the summer. Returning is a constant part of the human experience. When you return somewhere, there is a reason. When you return to work, you do it so you can provide financially. When you return to a favorite restaurant, you can enjoy their food. You return because there is something valuable in what you are returning to. I have worked so hard because soccer is so valuable to me. Even though playing soccer won't ever be the same, I realized that I am not returning, but I am starting again. I had to learn how to walk, then run, and know how to play soccer again.

annotations.text: I realized that I am not returning, but I am starting again. I had to learn how to walk, then run, and know how to play soccer again.

I'd like to see you take that opportunity and expand this essay's center of gravity, but--as it stands--this is a successful essay. Thanks for sharing it!
